# Diffusion Models {.section-divider}

## One Recipe, Many Data Types

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Diffusion models do not fundamentally care whether the sample is:

- an image
- a video
- a robot trajectory
- an action chunk

The object is represented as a tensor and modeled as a sample from a data distribution.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/tensor_modalities.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: start from the generality of the diffusion recipe before specializing to actions.

The useful insight from Vincenzo's lecture material is that diffusion operates on tensors. An image, a video, and a robot trajectory have different structure, but in each case we can represent the object as a point in a high-dimensional space.

For robotics, this matters because the model can generate not only pixels, but also vectors of joint commands, end-effector poses, target displacements, or controller parameters. The neural architecture changes with the structure of the tensor, but the noising and denoising idea remains the same.

Warning: this does not mean the semantics are irrelevant for the whole system. Semantics enter through the architecture, the conditioning information, the dataset, and the controller that executes the output.
:::

---

## Diffusion as Distribution Learning

Generation means sampling a structured object:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
x_0 \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)
$$
:::

Diffusion replaces direct sampling from $p_{\text{data}}$ with an easier path:

::: {.process-list}
1. define a fixed process that destroys structure with Gaussian noise
2. train a neural network to reverse that process
3. sample by iteratively denoising random noise
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: formalize generation as sampling from the data distribution.

We do not know the true distribution of demonstrations, images, or action chunks. We only have samples. A diffusion model learns a reverse process that starts from a distribution we can sample easily, usually a standard Gaussian, and gradually moves toward the data distribution.

This framing will later become a policy: instead of sampling an unconditional object, we sample an action trajectory conditioned on observations.
:::

---

## Forward and Reverse Processes

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
The forward process is fixed:

$$
q(x_k \mid x_{k-1})
=
\mathcal{N}
\left(
x_k;
\sqrt{1-\beta_k}x_{k-1},
\beta_k I
\right)
$$

The reverse process is learned:

$$
p_\theta(x_{k-1} \mid x_k, c)
$$
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/diffusion_forward_reverse.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: separate the fixed corruption process from the learned denoising process.

The forward process is not learned. We choose a schedule of noise variances and progressively corrupt the data. The reverse process is learned because the true reverse conditional depends on the unknown data distribution.

I use $k$ for diffusion time. This is not robot time. Robot time indexes physical execution, while diffusion time indexes the artificial denoising loop used by the generative model.

The conditioning variable $c$ will later be the robot observation history: images, proprioception, force signals, or task context.
:::

---

## Closed-Form Noising

By composing the forward steps, we can sample directly at any diffusion step:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
x_k
=
\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_k}x_0
+
\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_k}\epsilon,
\qquad
\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)
$$
:::

where

$$
\alpha_k = 1-\beta_k,
\qquad
\bar{\alpha}_k = \prod_{i=1}^{k}\alpha_i .
$$

::: {.takeaway}
Training does not require simulating all previous noising steps. We can draw $k$ and corrupt $x_0$ in one step.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: the closed-form forward marginal makes training efficient.

This is one of the most important practical facts. If we want a noisy sample at diffusion step $k$, we do not have to run the full chain from $0$ to $k$. We sample Gaussian noise once and mix it with the clean sample using $\bar{\alpha}_k$.

For action trajectories, $x_0$ is the expert action chunk. We corrupt that chunk and ask the network to predict the noise that was added.
:::

---

## Noise Prediction Objective

A standard training objective predicts the injected noise:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\mathcal{L}_{diff}(\theta)
=
\mathbb{E}_{x_0,\epsilon,k}
\left[
\lVert
\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_k, k, c)
\rVert^2
\right]
$$
:::

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- $x_0$: clean data sample or expert action chunk
- $x_k$: noisy version at diffusion step $k$
- $c$: optional conditioning information
- $\epsilon_\theta$: denoising network prediction
:::

::: {.warning}
The model is not trained to "undo robot noise." The noise is a learning construction used to fit a generative model.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: the model learns to estimate the noise component added during the forward process.

Once the model can estimate the noise in $x_k$, we can use that estimate to move the sample toward a cleaner point. The same loss is used whether $x$ is an image tensor or an action trajectory tensor.

There are other parameterizations, such as predicting $x_0$ or a velocity variable, but noise prediction is the standard formulation to introduce here.
:::

---

## Architecture Depends on Structure

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
The loss can stay the same while the denoising backbone changes:

- 2D U-Net for image tensors
- 1D U-Net for temporal action chunks
- Transformers for longer-range coupling

For Diffusion Policy, the key transition is from spatial denoising to sequence denoising.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/diffusion_architecture_bridge.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: motivate the 1D U-Net used later in Diffusion Policy.

The diffusion objective only says that the network receives a noisy sample and a diffusion step, then predicts noise of the same shape. It does not prescribe the architecture.

The architecture should match the structure of the data. Images have local spatial structure, so a 2D U-Net is natural. Action trajectories have temporal structure, so a 1D U-Net with convolutions along the horizon is natural. Transformers can be useful when longer-range interactions dominate, but they usually require more data and compute.
:::

---

## Sampling Bottleneck

Sampling starts from Gaussian noise and applies the denoiser repeatedly:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
x_K \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I),
\qquad
x_K \rightarrow x_{K-1} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow x_0
$$
:::

- More denoising steps usually give better refinement.
- Each step is a neural network evaluation.
- Robot policies must respect a control-loop timing budget.

::: {.notes}
Main point: introduce the computational bottleneck before discussing DDPM and DDIM.

This is where diffusion models become different from a standard feed-forward policy. A deterministic policy typically performs one forward pass per control query. A diffusion policy may require tens or hundreds of denoising passes to produce one action chunk.

For offline image generation, this may be acceptable. For robot control, latency and jitter affect closed-loop behavior, tracking error, and contact response.
:::

---

## DDPM vs DDIM

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
**DDPM**

- stochastic reverse process
- many small denoising steps
- conceptually close to the probabilistic derivation

**DDIM**

- deterministic or partially stochastic updates
- fewer denoising steps
- attractive for real-time policy inference
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/ddpm_ddim_comparison.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: distinguish DDPM and DDIM in terms of sampling behavior and control relevance.

DDPM sampling adds stochasticity at reverse steps. DDIM constructs a non-Markovian reverse trajectory that can skip many intermediate timesteps while preserving the same training objective.

For robotics, the practical question is not only sample quality. It is whether the sampled action chunk is good enough, stable enough, and fast enough for closed-loop deployment.
:::
